TextENGNew: On 'Homing Instinct' and 'Homesickness'
If we treat 'homing instinct' in its broadest sense, it can be said that virtually all living creatures possess one.
The same applies to humans. We prefer to sleep, rest, and eat in familiar surroundings—that is to say, at home.
This 'home' corresponds directly to the concept of a nest.@k Thus, we can say that humans, too, possess a homing instinct.
Why do animals need nests in the first place?
The answer is exactly the same as for humans.
The acts of sleeping, resting, and eating are essential for survival, yet they are simultaneously acts that leave one vulnerable and exposed.
For this reason, it became necessary to establish a 'safe zone' where one could afford to let down one's guard.
Everyone knows that the absence of such a 'safe zone' inflicts severe stress.

Force an animal into an unfamiliar environment, and it will experience considerable stress.@k Force a human to live in an unfamiliar land where they can never relax, and the result is equally severe stress.
Everyone is familiar with the name given to this stress: 'homesickness.'
In Japan, research on 'homesickness' has been remarkably shallow.
changeReason: Old says 'The Japanese don't know much about homesickness' (overly generalized); new says 'research on homesickness has been remarkably shallow' matching the Japanese 研究が非常に浅い (research is very shallow).

TextENGNew: This is because, for a long time, it was dismissed as a weakness of spirit, and its treatment was neglected.
'Homesickness' has a very long history. In medieval Europe, it was believed to be not a mental affliction but a physical disease.
Far from their homeland, with no means—such as the telephone of the modern age—to hear the voices of those they'd left behind.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: It is not hard to imagine that young people working in such foreign lands would succumb to 'homesickness.'
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: Those afflicted with 'homesickness' became overwhelmed by an irresistible longing to return home.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: When the conflict arose between this desire and the obligations of their work, which prevented them from going back, they deteriorated both physically and mentally, sometimes to the point of psychological breakdown, driven to acts of flight such as desertion or suicide.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: People of that era believed in this superstition as well, and a great many folk remedies and quasi-magical cures meant to ward off 'homesickness' gained wide popularity.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: Since there was no treatment other than returning to one's homeland, it might well have been considered an incurable disease.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: However, calling it a disease is not strictly accurate.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: More precisely, 'homesickness' can be described as a homing signal—the brain's demand to return to the 'safe zone' of one's homeland.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: Initially, the brain stimulates memories of home, encouraging a natural return.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: But when reason overrides this urge and prevents the homecoming, the brain begins sending abnormal signals to the body.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: The result is physical decline, which makes life in the foreign land increasingly untenable, until finally reason itself is bent, and the return home is achieved.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: In short, 'homesickness' is produced by the 'homing instinct.'
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: And by the same token, a stronger 'homing instinct' produces more powerful 'homesickness.'
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: The strength of the 'homing instinct' naturally varies between individuals and is greatly influenced by one's environment.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: If one's homeland is perceived as safe and one's current environment as dangerous, the 'homing instinct' will be strong.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: Conversely, if one's current environment is safer than one's homeland, the 'homing instinct' will likely be quite weak.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: Moreover, it is not simply a matter of safety; cultural factors also play a role.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: The customs demanding periodic homecomings vary by region, and the culture of one's birthplace presumably influences the strength of the 'homing instinct' as well.
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: Within this context, the 'transcendents' of Onigafuchi Village stand out as possessing an extraordinarily potent 'homing instinct.'
changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

changeReason: Old translation was shifted/misaligned with the Japanese source text; new correctly corresponds to the Japanese line.

TextENGNew: If the relationship between 'homing instinct' and 'homesickness' is proportional,@k then the extravagant expression 'curse' is itself an indicator of the intensity of the 'homesickness,' and by extension, the intensity of the 'homing instinct.'
Could it be that this very intensity of 'homing instinct' is what led them to so fiercely abhor the outside world and construct their insular society?
In ancient Onigafuchi Village, unfortunately, there are no cases by which to measure the strength of the 'homing instinct.'
Because leaving the village was so strictly forbidden by law, there simply were no instances of anyone being away long enough to develop 'homesickness.'
However, from the Meiji era onward, the situation changed dramatically.
The tide of modernization swept in, and Onigafuchi Village lost its sanctity.
More and more young people yearned for the city, and some were compelled to leave the village to find employment.
The result was an exodus of villagers.
Here, for the first time, the descendants of Onigafuchi's transcendents came to understand the true strength of their own 'homing instinct.'
The residents of Hinamizawa experienced 'homesickness' of an abnormal intensity.
Before long, this 'homesickness' came to be called, by one and all, 'the curse of Oyashiro-sama.'

When a rumor spread that a migrant worker had encountered the curse in a distant land and died under bizarre circumstances, the expatriate villagers returned one after another—to the village or to nearby towns.
changeReason: Old and new translations convey substantially different content; the new translation more closely reflects the Japanese source text.

TextENGNew: This intensity of 'homesickness' and 'homing instinct' was presumably cultivated by their culture—@k namely, the Oyashiro-sama faith unique to Hinamizawa. That would be the most reasonable interpretation.
From the moment of their birth, they must have been subconsciously imprinted with Oyashiro-sama's laws, and must have felt an unconscious sense of sin at the very thought of leaving the village.
As a result, they interpreted their 'homesickness' as 'the curse of Oyashiro-sama.'
Did Oyashiro-sama's laws intensify the 'homing instinct,' or did an already overpowering 'homesickness' give rise to Oyashiro-sama's laws?
...Which came first remains unclear.
...Tired of reading such small print, I flopped onto my back and stared up at the ceiling.
The true identity of 'the curse of Oyashiro-sama' is... homesickness?
I'd expected something different from the scrapbooks of Miyo-san—the one person who'd understood my story—and read them eagerly.
...So I was rather put off to find that the whole thing boiled down to 'homesickness'—the very word my counselors and doctors had repeated to me so many times.
How could a word like 'homesickness' possibly explain what I experienced?
I saw Oyashiro-sama descend in that blindingly radiant bathroom with my own eyes.
I saw those filthy, dark reddish-black things bubbling out of my blood vessels.@k None of that was explained here.

TextENGNew: During the mass homecoming of expatriate residents in the Meiji era, a rumor of a certain abnormal phenomenon circulated among the returnees.

TextENGNew: On 'Parasites' and Host Manipulation

Reflecting on the abnormal phenomenon experienced by the group of returnees, I began to wonder: could Hinamizawa's excessive 'homesickness' be, in fact, an infectious condition caused by some kind of parasite?
changeReason: Old translation uses 'maggots' while new translation uses more general terms; the Japanese text uses words like 得体の知れないもの (unidentifiable things) or 虫 (worms/bugs), not specifically 'maggots.'

TextENGNew: This is not because they saw unidentifiable things emerging from their wounds.@k Rather, I wish to explore the possibility that what they have always considered their culture—their faith—is itself connected to an infectious agent.
changeReason: Old translation uses 'maggots' while new translation uses more general terms; the Japanese text uses words like 得体の知れないもの (unidentifiable things) or 虫 (worms/bugs), not specifically 'maggots.'

TextENGNew: Most infectious diseases are caused unintentionally by parasites.
For a parasite, the host is quite literally its home.
Therefore, the ultimate form of parasitism would be perfect symbiosis—coexistence so seamless that the host never becomes aware of the intruder.
changeReason: Old and new translations convey substantially different content; the new translation more closely reflects the Japanese source text.

TextENGNew: However, parasites sometimes manipulate their hosts in order to exploit them for their own propagation.

For instance, among parasites that spread via intermediate hosts,@k there is a well-documented ability to control the host's behavior in order to facilitate deliberate predation by the subsequent host, thereby achieving transmission.

A classic example is Dicrocoelium dendriticum, which uses snails and ants as intermediate hosts, guiding the intermediate host to the tips of grass blades to be consumed by sheep, the definitive host.
For manipulation of more complex animals, there is also the example of Toxoplasma gondii, which uses mice as intermediate hosts with cats as the definitive host.
In this case, it is known that the parasite eliminates the intermediate host's aversion to cats, thereby increasing the probability of predation and transmission.
changeReason: Old and new translations convey substantially different content; the new translation more closely reflects the Japanese source text.

TextENGNew: There are also cases of indiscriminate infection aimed at an unspecified range of hosts.
A particularly famous example is the rabies virus.
It parasitizes and reproduces within the host's brain, then migrates via the salivary glands to mix with the saliva.

Simultaneously, it dramatically heightens the host's aggression, causing it to bite indiscriminately, and the virus enters through the wound mixed with saliva.@k ...Incidentally, this is what we know as rabies.
changeReason: Old and new translations convey substantially different content; the new translation more closely reflects the Japanese source text.

TextENGNew: As these examples show, a considerable number of parasites possess the capacity to manipulate their hosts for their own benefit.

Now, most of the examples cited above involve parasites manipulating hosts for the purpose of propagation and transmission.@k But might there not also be cases where parasites manipulate host behavior simply to secure a more comfortable environment for themselves?
Here, I wish to explore whether this concept can be linked to the abnormal 'homing instinct' and 'homesickness' of Onigafuchi Village's residents, which I proposed earlier.
Let us hypothesize that a certain species of parasite is endemic to Hinamizawa and has infected every villager.
changeReason: Old and new translations convey substantially different content; the new translation more closely reflects the Japanese source text.

TextENGNew: For these parasites, Hinamizawa represents the most hospitable environment,@k and they instill in their hosts a powerful 'homing instinct' to keep them rooted in this land.
When the hosts ignore this and attempt to leave, the parasites resist by triggering 'homesickness.'
Parasites, as the name implies, cannot survive without a host.
For them, it would be most convenient for their human hosts to establish a community and remain permanently in the environment best suited to the parasites.
If we hypothesize the existence of parasites that seek to bind their hosts to their preferred territory, it becomes surprisingly easy to explain the mysteries discussed thus far.
changeReason: Old and new translations convey substantially different content; the new translation more closely reflects the Japanese source text.

TextENGNew: That is, the ancient transcendents of Onigafuchi Village knew of these parasites' existence.
Knowing that they, as hosts, could not survive outside the village, they created laws and a culture that shunned the outside world.
This would also explain the abnormal fervor of the dam conflict in recent years.
If so... could the demons said to have poured from the swamp in the legend of Oyashiro-sama actually represent a mass outbreak of these parasites?
The accounts of demons attacking villagers most likely describe the frenzied behavior of end-stage infected individuals.
...Admittedly, this was so outlandish that I wanted to laugh it off.
But I felt it would be disrespectful to Miyo-san, so I held back.
This Hinamizawa... is actually controlled by parasites...?
If I considered only those strange things that had overflowed from my wounds, it wasn't impossible to make such a connection.
changeReason: Old translation uses 'maggots' while new translation uses more general terms; the Japanese text uses words like 得体の知れないもの (unidentifiable things) or 虫 (worms/bugs), not specifically 'maggots.'
